primary_mitair_label: 2.1 Compromise of privacy by obtaining, leaking or correctly inferring sensitive information
secondary_mitair_label: 2.2 AI system security vulnerabilities and attacks
why_this_bridge_is_useful_now: The largest current LIMEN harm label already aligns with one visible MIT AI Risk privacy subdomain and one adjacent security subdomain, which is enough for a bounded concept-layer bridge in methods and taxonomy views.
overread_to_avoid: Do not treat this as event validation, row-level join evidence, or proof that all privacy rows are semantically identical.
paper_thesis_use: Methods appendix, taxonomy heatmap legend, and thesis note on concept-only external alignment for privacy-heavy rows.
visualization_dashboard_hook: Top-harm-to-MITAIR bridge panel; taxonomy heatmap sidecar; residual-category overlay.
next_smallest_publishability_move: Promote this row into one shared manuscript or dashboard surface before reopening MIT AI Risk for more anchor capture.

why_this_bridge_is_useful_now: This common LIMEN harm label does not map cleanly to one single MIT AI Risk object, but the visible governance-failure and transparency/interpretability anchors give a reviewer-safe concept pair for accountability-heavy rows.
overread_to_avoid: Do not present governance failure or lack of transparency as a one-to-one replacement for LIMEN accountability coding or as a legal-compliance conclusion.
paper_thesis_use: Interpretive methods note for accountability-heavy cases, plus thesis language on why some LIMEN labels require paired external anchors rather than a single exact match.
visualization_dashboard_hook: Accountability bridge inset with paired-anchor badge and caution tooltip.
next_smallest_publishability_move: Reuse only as a paired concept bridge; if a stronger shared need appears, test whether one sub-cluster can be separated instead of forcing one exact MITAIR slot.

primary_mitair_label: 2.2 AI system security vulnerabilities and attacks
secondary_mitair_label: 4.3 Cyberattacks, weapons development or use and mass harm
why_this_bridge_is_useful_now: The current security slice already fits the visible MIT AI Risk security-vulnerabilities anchor well, with a malicious-use secondary anchor available for rows where offensive or exploit-enabling conduct matters.
overread_to_avoid: Do not inflate this into prevalence, exploit severity, or broad malicious-actor claims across all security rows.
paper_thesis_use: Security-to-taxonomy bridge note, reviewer-safe comparator sidecar, and thesis methods example for exact-security-domain concept reuse.
visualization_dashboard_hook: Security taxonomy bridge panel; Table 6A concept-overlay companion; graph-ready harm-domain edge list.
next_smallest_publishability_move: Promote the primary security anchor into one shared taxonomy surface and keep the malicious-use anchor secondary unless a row-level sub-cluster is deliberately split.

harm_label: uncertain or no realized harm but credible hazard
why_this_bridge_is_useful_now: The label is common in LIMEN but inherently mixed; the visible MIT AI Risk safety/limitations anchors give a bounded way to explain why the atlas records credible hazards separately from realized harms.
overread_to_avoid: Do not collapse credible hazard, dangerous capability, and realized incident harm into one count or one row-level identity claim.
paper_thesis_use: Methods paragraph on hazard-versus-realized-harm handling, plus dashboard legend text explaining why some rows remain important before realized damage is public.
visualization_dashboard_hook: Hazard-versus-realized-harm toggle; safety-limitations sidecar; reviewer caution legend.
next_smallest_publishability_move: Use only in methods/legend surfaces unless a later shared figure explicitly distinguishes hazard-coded rows from realized-harm rows.

why_this_bridge_is_useful_now: The smaller finance/deception slice already has a clear visible MIT AI Risk fraud/manipulation anchor, with misinformation as a secondary concept where false claims or deceptive outputs drive the loss pathway.
overread_to_avoid: Do not generalize this into sector-wide financial-harm prevalence or substitute it for authority-graded enforcement evidence.
paper_thesis_use: Methods/table note for finance and deception rows, plus thesis explanation of why some market-facing harms map better to malicious-actor concepts than to safety or governance anchors.
visualization_dashboard_hook: Finance/deception edge list; fraud-manipulation badge; market-harm inset.
next_smallest_publishability_move: If this bridge is reused, keep the primary anchor on fraud/manipulation and only invoke misinformation where the row's evidence text actually supports it.

primary_mitair_label: 3.2 Pollution of information ecosystem and loss of consensus reality
secondary_mitair_label: 4.1 Disinformation, surveillance, and influence at scale
why_this_bridge_is_useful_now: This smaller but publication-distinct slice now has one visible misinformation/public-sphere anchor and one malicious-actor/public-influence anchor, which is enough for a bounded democracy-facing concept bridge.
overread_to_avoid: Do not restate this bridge as proof of electoral impact, democratic injury, or official causation beyond the underlying authoritative case evidence.
paper_thesis_use: Policy-facing appendix note, democracy/provenance methods paragraph, and thesis discussion of public-trust harms.
visualization_dashboard_hook: Public-trust bridge legend; democracy/provenance inset; map-ready concept overlay.
next_smallest_publishability_move: Use this pair only where the underlying LIMEN row already has strong public-authority or enforcement support for the event itself.
